title: Refund Cycling Volume Inflation with Rapid Recharge Pattern
narrative: Fraudster initiates legitimate-appearing purchases of $45-$85 digital goods (ProductCD=W or C), immediately requests refunds through customer service channels, then within 15-45 minutes uses the same card to make identical purchases. This creates artificial transaction velocity while maintaining near-zero net spend. Over 6-8 hours, the actor completes 12-18 purchase-refund-repurchase cycles on the same card. The pattern inflates card1_txn_count_7day from baseline 2-3 to 30+ while card1_net_amt_24h remains under $200. Refunds occur at MIDs different from purchase MIDs but same merchant umbrella. Goal is to age the card rapidly, establish transaction history for later fraud, or exploit velocity-blind authorization systems that don't weight refunded transactions appropriately in real-time decisioning.
indicators: 70%+ of transactions are refunds (TransactionDT gaps of 15-45 min between purchase and corresponding refund)
TransactionAmt variance under $25 across all purchase events (tight clustering)
Alternating TransactionDT pattern: purchase at T, refund at T+20min, purchase at T+35min, refund at T+55min
Same P_emaildomain and addr1 across all cycles but rotating MID values
card1_amt_zscore_24h exceeds 3.5 despite low net amount
ProductCD consistency (all W or all C) with identical item descriptions in metadata
summary: A single card shows 12+ transactions in 6 hours but the net spend stays under $250 because most transactions are refunds that occur 15-45 minutes after each purchase, all for similar amounts ($45-$85) and the same product type.
reasoning: The fraudster is artificially aging the card and building transaction history through rapid purchase-refund-repurchase cycles, either to prepare the card for larger fraud later or to exploit systems that don't properly account for refunds when checking velocity limits.
indicator_explanations: Card runs through a dozen transactions in six hours with minimal net spending
Most transactions are refunds happening within minutes of the original purchase
Purchase amounts stay tightly bunched in a narrow dollar range
Transactions follow a mechanical buy-refund-buy-refund rhythm with predictable timing
Email and address stay constant while merchant identifiers keep rotating
Transaction volume is abnormally high compared to card's typical daily behavior
Product type and descriptions are suspiciously identical across all purchases
